explanation: The chat app where people set dramatic screen names and spammed nudges.
The collectible cardboard milk caps kids stacked and slammed on the playground.
The famously indestructible cell phone with a battery that seemed to last forever.
The simple but addictive game on old Nokia phones.
America Online Instant Messenger, where buddy lists and away messages ruled.
The digital pet keychain you had to feed before it died.
The file-sharing service that blew up the music industry.
The portable CD player that skipped if you moved too fast.
The noisy box that screamed before connecting you to the internet.
The kids' TV channel behind slime, cartoons, and after-school obsession.
The channel that shaped music videos, reality TV, and teen culture.
The British girl group that made 'Girl Power' a global slogan.
The hyper-fast, high-energy dance music of the late 90s.
The fuzzy toy that looked cute until it started talking in the middle of the night.
The handheld that finally let your Nintendo games show color.
The trading cards every kid argued over at lunch, with Charizard as the dream pull.
The colorful string bracelets kids knotted for each other at sleepovers and camp.
The explosion of neon folders, stickers, and school supplies covered in rainbow animals.
The velour tracksuit brand that defined early-2000s celebrity style.
The square storage disk now mostly remembered as the save icon.
The portable cassette player clipped to your belt or stuffed in your backpack.
The movie format you had to rewind before returning.
The video rental store that used to own Friday night.
The lifeguard show famous for red swimsuits and slow-motion running.
The sitcom that turned Will Smith into a TV star.
The defining 90s sitcom about six friends hanging out in New York.
The reality format that locked strangers in a house and changed TV forever.
The singing competition that turned Simon Cowell into a household name.
The music-downloading program that also loved giving your computer viruses.
The file-sharing app that became a Napster-era classic.
Apple's white music player with the click wheel that changed portable listening.
The Princess of Pop who dominated late-90s and early-2000s music.
One of the biggest boy bands of the era.
Justin Timberlake's boy band before his solo career took over.
The life sim where players built houses and trapped Sims in swimming pools.
The game where you built theme parks and wildly unsafe coasters.
The turn-based game where cartoon worms attacked each other with ridiculous weapons.
The unbearably cheerful Microsoft Office paperclip assistant.
The curvy, rainbow-colored text every school project somehow needed.
The absurdly wide denim legs that screamed late-90s style.
The trucker-hat brand that briefly took over early-2000s celebrity fashion.
Shoes with wheels in the heels so kids could roll through stores and hallways.
The strange stretchy goo that bounced and picked up newspaper ink.
The glittery, neon pens that filled every pencil case.
The backpack brand that felt like it was in every middle and high school hallway.
The girl group that launched Beyonce into superstardom.
The boy band created on reality TV with the hit 'All or Nothing.'
The reality show about building pop groups and surviving music industry chaos.
The hip-hop duo behind huge crossover hits like 'Hey Ya!'
The rapper whose rise defined a big part of early-2000s pop culture.
The cartoon about a teen girl whose braces caused constant chaos.
The wildly quotable cartoon sponge who ruled 2000s kids TV.
The anime where battles lasted forever and everyone chased the next power-up.
The reality show built around testing relationships with obvious bad ideas.
The teen drama that made California angst feel glamorous.
The show where celebrities gave tours of their ridiculously over-the-top houses.
The show where Xzibit turned junky cars into rolling gimmicks.
Johnny Knoxville and friends turning pain into entertainment.
The pixelated online hangout where kids decorated rooms and chatted for hours.
The browser RPG where an entire generation chopped wood and trained skills.
The social network where people customized their profile with bad HTML and a Top 8.
The dashboard GPS device people relied on before phones handled navigation.
The school event that felt like a magical store had suddenly appeared in the library.
The stuffed animals people collected like they were going to fund retirement.
The teen magazine packed with celebrity gossip and bedroom posters.
The doll line and magazine universe that became a full identity for a lot of kids.
The classic schoolyard game built around tiny glass balls and playground rules.
The spinning-top battle toy that took over recess.
The card game that became the next playground obsession after Pokemon.
The deeply annoying ringtone mascot that somehow became a huge hit.
The viral webcam lip-sync clip from the very early internet meme era.
The trio behind the unavoidable dance hit 'The Ketchup Song.'
The party dance absolutely everyone was expected to know.
The era when using the internet tied up the phone line.
The virtual pet website that turned feeding digital creatures into a daily routine.
The dolls with oversized heads, heavy makeup, and huge early-2000s attitude.
The Dutch economist who won the first Nobel Prize in Economics and helped found econometrics.
The standard fitting method that minimizes the squared residuals, usually referred to as OLS.
When the variance of the error term is not constant across observations.
When two or more independent variables in a model are strongly correlated with each other.
When an explanatory variable is correlated with the error term, which can bias estimates.
Variables used to deal with endogeneity when direct OLS estimation is not valid.
When error terms are correlated across time or across ordered observations.
The property of a time series whose statistical behavior stays stable over time.
The share of variation in the dependent variable explained by the model.
The probability of seeing a result this extreme if the null hypothesis were true.
The threshold, often 5%, below which a result is treated as statistically meaningful.
The theorem stating that OLS is BLUE under the standard linear model assumptions.
A regression model for binary outcomes based on the logistic distribution.
A regression model for binary outcomes based on the normal distribution.
A model used when the dependent variable is censored, such as being piled up at zero.
Data that follows the same units, such as people or firms, across multiple time periods.
A classic time-series model used for analyzing and forecasting data over time.
A model commonly used in finance to capture changing volatility over time.
A statistical test used to check whether a time series has a unit root.
A way to test whether one time series helps predict another.
A method that repeatedly simulates data to study how an estimator or model behaves.
An estimation method that chooses the parameter values that make the observed data most likely.
An approach that combines prior beliefs with observed data to estimate model parameters.
Generalized Method of Moments, a flexible estimation framework based on moment conditions.
A sequence of random shocks with mean zero, constant variance, and no serial correlation.
A variable that takes the value 0 or 1 to represent a category.
A regression method that shrinks coefficients and can set some of them exactly to zero.
A causal inference method that compares changes over time between treated and control groups.
A measure of the uncertainty around an estimated parameter.
The test used to check whether a single coefficient differs significantly from zero.
The test used to check whether a group of variables is jointly significant.
When a variable is independent of the error term in the model.
A sudden change in the underlying relationship described by a model.
The percentage change in one variable caused by a 1% change in another.
The Mother of Dragons who saw herself as the rightful heir to the Iron Throne.
The bastard of Winterfell who turned out to be Aegon Targaryen.
The sharp-tongued Lannister known for his wit, wine, and political survival.
The jagged royal seat forged from the swords of defeated enemies.
The ancestral castle of House Stark and the heart of the North.
The giant wall of ice meant to keep out White Walkers and wildlings.
The sworn brotherhood that guards the Wall in black.
The undead ice creatures led by the Night King.
Daenerys's largest dragon, named after Khal Drogo.
The dragon who was killed and raised again as an ice dragon.
Daenerys's green dragon, named after her brother Rhaegar.
The massacre at the Freys where Robb Stark and his allies were slaughtered.
Rare magical steel that can kill White Walkers.
The ruthless queen who would do anything to keep power and protect her children.
The Kingslayer who killed Aerys and spent the series chasing redemption.
The Stark girl who trained with the Faceless Men and killed the Night King.
Arya's thin sword, given to her by Jon Snow.
The Stark daughter who became Queen in the North.
The Three-Eyed Raven who can see across the past and present.
The gentle giant whose name came from the phrase 'Hold the door.'
The sadistic Bolton bastard who tortured Theon into Reek.
The cruel boy king who died at his own wedding.
The master manipulator better known as Littlefinger.
The nickname of Petyr Baelish, the schemer who said chaos is a ladder.
The Spider, whose network of little birds kept him informed on every court secret.
Gregor Clegane, the giant knight who became Cersei's silent undead enforcer.
Sandor Clegane, the scarred fighter with a brutal streak and a hidden conscience.
The fiercely loyal warrior who was one of the best fighters in Westeros.
The Red Priestess who served the Lord of Light.
Robert's stern younger brother who believed the throne was his by right.
The Red Viper of Dorne who died fighting the Mountain.
The Queen of Thorns with the sharpest tongue in Westeros.
The Stark ward who betrayed them, was broken by Ramsay, and died redeeming himself.
High Valyrian for 'all men must die.'
The response to Valar Morghulis, meaning 'all men must serve.'
The assassin cult in Braavos that can take on other faces.
The Free City known for the Titan and the Iron Bank.
The capital of the Seven Kingdoms and the seat of the Iron Throne.
The bright green liquid that burns even on water.
The author of A Song of Ice and Fire, the book series behind the show.
The horse-riding warrior people from the plains of Essos.
The command Daenerys uses to make her dragons breathe fire.
Ned Stark, the honorable lord whose execution kicked off the war.
The king's chief adviser and most powerful official.
The main stronghold of the Night's Watch at the base of the Wall.
The harsh island home of House Greyjoy and the Drowned God.
The words of House Stark and the series' most famous warning.
The headmaster of Hogwarts, owner of the Elder Wand, and the wizard Voldemort feared most.
The school of witchcraft and wizardry hidden in a Scottish castle.
An object or living thing that contains a piece of a dark wizard's soul.
The three legendary objects: the Elder Wand, Resurrection Stone, and Invisibility Cloak.
The unbeatable wand and one of the Deathly Hallows.
The Hallow that can bring back shades of the dead.
Harry's heirloom cloak that truly hides its wearer.
Voldemort's real name, which he hated.
The Half-Blood Prince, Potions master, and one of the saga's most complicated figures.
Harry's brilliant best friend, known for always having the answer.
Harry's loyal red-haired best friend from the Weasley family.
The awkward student who grows into a hero and kills Nagini.
The dreamy Ravenclaw who believes in all kinds of strange creatures.
Harry's school rival and the son of Death Eater Lucius Malfoy.
Harry's godfather, wrongly imprisoned in Azkaban for years.
The werewolf teacher and one of the Marauders.
The magical map of Hogwarts created by Moony, Wormtail, Padfoot, and Prongs.
The hidden shopping street in London for witches and wizards.
The darker side street off Diagon Alley where shady magic is sold.
The wizarding bank run by goblins.
The only all-wizarding village in Britain.
The crooked magical home of the Weasley family.
The Dursley address where Harry grows up under the stairs.
The devoted followers of Lord Voldemort.
The secret group formed by Dumbledore to oppose Voldemort.
Harry's secret defense group for students when Umbridge takes over.
The spell used to conjure a Patronus against Dementors.
The Killing Curse and one of the Unforgivable Curses.
The torture curse and one of the Unforgivable Curses.
The soul-sucking guards of Azkaban.
The winged horses visible only to people who have seen death.
A magical servant creature that can be freed by receiving clothes.
The house-elf who wins his freedom and becomes fiercely loyal to Harry.
The potion that lets you take on someone else's appearance for a while.
The basin where memories can be stored and rewatched.
The enchanted hat that sorts new students into houses.
The Hogwarts house associated with bravery and a lion.
The Hogwarts house associated with ambition, cunning, and a serpent.
The Hogwarts house known for intelligence and learning.
The Hogwarts house known for loyalty and hard work.
The wizarding sport played on broomsticks with four balls.
The tiny winged ball the Seeker tries to catch to end the match.
The red ball Chasers throw through the hoops to score.
One of the heavy black balls that tries to knock players off their brooms.
The Quidditch position responsible for catching the Snitch.
Harry's first top-of-the-line racing broom.
A person with no magical ability.
A nasty slur for a witch or wizard born to Muggle parents.
The rare ability to speak with snakes.
The Transfiguration professor and head of Gryffindor.
The gamekeeper of Hogwarts who adores dangerous creatures.
The giant serpent hidden in the Chamber of Secrets.
The hidden chamber built by Salazar Slytherin beneath Hogwarts.
The room that appears only when someone truly needs it.
The hidden King's Cross platform where the Hogwarts Express departs.
The red steam train that takes students to school.
The governing body of the British wizarding world.
A dark wizard catcher who works for the Ministry.
The wizard prison guarded by Dementors.
The pink-clad Ministry enforcer everyone loves to hate.
The grumpy Hogwarts caretaker and a Squib.
The ghost of Gryffindor House.
The frightening ghost of Slytherin House.
The ghost who haunts the bathroom where the Basilisk killed her.
The Minister of Magic who refused to accept Voldemort's return.
The tougher Minister who takes over after Fudge.
The teacher who runs the Slug Club and knows about Horcruxes.
The major exams Hogwarts students take in their fifth year.
The advanced exams taken at the end of seventh year.
The favorite warm drink in Hogsmeade.
The candy beans that can taste like anything from strawberry to earwax.
The candy that comes with a moving card of a famous witch or wizard.
Madam Rosmerta's inn in Hogsmeade.
The dark wizard Dumbledore defeated in 1945.
The alchemist who made the Sorcerer's Stone.
The magical object that chooses champions for the Triwizard Tournament.
The Bulgarian Seeker and Durmstrang champion in the Triwizard Tournament.
The Beauxbatons champion who later marries Bill Weasley.
The magical art of teleporting from one place to another.
The purple triple-decker bus for stranded witches and wizards.
The wandmaker behind 'the wand chooses the wizard.'
The Knockturn Alley shop that sells dark magical objects.
The violent tree planted on the Hogwarts grounds.
The supposedly haunted building in Hogsmeade tied to Lupin.
The proud magical creature that is half eagle and half horse.
Hagrid's hippogriff, sentenced to death before being rescued.
The giant Acromantula living in the Forbidden Forest.
The fanatical Death Eater who kills Sirius Black.
Draco's aristocratic father and a major Death Eater.
Draco's mother, whose lie helps save Harry in the end.
The Malfoy estate that becomes Voldemort's headquarters.
The summoning spell.
The disarming charm and Harry's signature spell.
The unlocking spell.
The spell that creates light at the tip of a wand.
The spell that turns wand light off.
Harry's father and one of the Marauders.
Harry's aunt and Lily Potter's bitter sister.
Harry's blustering Muggle uncle.
Harry's spoiled cousin.
Ron's pet rat, who is really Peter Pettigrew.
The traitor who betrayed Harry's parents to Voldemort.
Harry's loyal snowy owl.
The giant three-headed dog guarding the trapdoor in the first book.
Voldemort's snake and one of his Horcruxes.
The Weasley mother who defeats Bellatrix in the final battle.
The oldest Weasley brother, who works for Gringotts.
The Weasley brother who works with dragons in Romania.
The ambitious Weasley brother who works for the Ministry.
The prankster twins who leave Hogwarts in a blaze of fireworks.
The youngest Weasley and Harry's future wife.
The Ring of Fire, one of the three Elven Rings, worn by Gandalf.
The Ring of Water, worn by Galadriel in Lothlorien.
The Ring of Air, the mightiest of the three Elven Rings, worn by Elrond.
The Elven smith who forged the Rings of Power.
The last High King of the Noldor, killed in the Last Alliance.
The mighty Elf lord who rescues Frodo in the book version.
The lord of the Grey Havens who gave Narya to Gandalf.
The Elven king of Mirkwood and Legolas's father.
Galadriel's husband and lord of Lothlorien.
The Elf of Lothlorien who guides the Fellowship.
Elrond's daughter, the Evenstar, who chose a mortal life for Aragorn.
The lord of Rivendell and keeper of Vilya.
The Balrog of Moria that slept beneath Khazad-dum.
The Dwarven name for Moria.
The Dwarf king from the Iron Hills who later ruled under the Mountain.
The dwarf whose failed colony in Moria is discovered by the Fellowship.
The axe-wielding dwarf of the Fellowship.
The dwarf leader in The Hobbit who sought Erebor and the Arkenstone.
Aragorn's sword, reforged from the shards of Narsil.
The sword with which Isildur cut the Ring from Sauron's hand.
Bilbo and Frodo's glowing Elven blade.
Gandalf's famous sword, also called Foe-hammer.
One of the seeing-stones used to communicate across great distances.
The royal name Aragorn takes when he becomes king.
The strange and merry being whom the Ring does not affect.
The man who took the One Ring instead of destroying it.
The first Dark Lord and Sauron's original master.
The giant spider who stings Frodo near Cirith Ungol.
The nine Ringwraiths who serve Sauron.
The only Nazgul besides the Witch-king named by Tolkien.
The envoy of Barad-dur who speaks for Sauron at the Black Gate.
The head of the wizards who falls under Sauron's influence.
The wizard more concerned with animals and nature than power.
The two mysterious wizards who went east and vanished from the main story.
Wormtongue, the treacherous adviser who poisons Theoden's court.
The king of Rohan who rides to war at Helm's Deep and Pelennor.
Theoden's nephew and later king of Rohan.
The shieldmaiden of Rohan who kills the Witch-king.
The troubled Steward of Gondor and father of Boromir and Faramir.
The younger son of Denethor who resists the lure of the Ring.
The Gondorian warrior who dies defending Merry and Pippin.
The guard of Minas Tirith who helps save Faramir.
The hobbit who looks into the palantir and serves Gondor.
The hobbit who helps Eowyn bring down the Witch-king.
Frodo's loyal gardener and the emotional core of the quest.
The Ring-bearer who carries the One Ring to Mount Doom.
The hobbit who found the Ring in The Hobbit.
Gollum's original name before the Ring twisted him.
The relative Smeagol murdered after Deagol found the Ring.
The oldest Ent and leader of the Ents.
The lord of horses who bears Gandalf.
The White City and capital of Gondor.
The corrupted city once known as Minas Ithil.
Sauron's dark fortress in Mordor.
The ringed fortress where Orthanc stands.
The black tower at the center of Isengard.
The golden Elven realm ruled by Galadriel and Celeborn.
The western port from which the Elves sail into the West.
The drowned island kingdom from which Aragorn's line descends.
The great king of men and father of Isildur.
The Blessed Realm in the far West.
The rare silver metal that is light, beautiful, and incredibly strong.
Elvish waybread that can sustain a traveler for a long journey.
The town where the hobbits first meet Strider.
Aragorn's ranger nickname in the North.
The descendants of Numenor, including Aragorn's people.
The larger, stronger breed of orcs bred by Saruman.
The massive battering ram used against Minas Tirith.
The giant war beasts ridden by the Haradrim.
The ruined former capital of Gondor on the Anduin.
The heavily guarded entrance to Mordor.
The pass and tower watched over by Shelob.
The haunted road Aragorn takes to summon the Dead Men of Dunharrow.
The cursed dead who broke their promise to Isildur.
Powerful spirits, including Gandalf, Sauron, and the Balrogs.
The greater divine powers who shape the world before the story begins.
The giant river statues of Isildur and Anarion.
The hill where the Fellowship breaks apart.
The broad name for the corrupted foot soldiers of evil.
Another name Tolkien often uses for orcs, especially in The Hobbit.
The giant evil wolves used as mounts by orcs.
The movie-original Uruk-hai who kills Boromir.
The hill where Frodo is stabbed by a Nazgul blade.
The healing herb also called kingsfoil.
The peaceful homeland of the hobbits.
Bilbo and Frodo's home in Hobbiton.
The hobbit region east of the Brandywine River.
The name for the Elves who began the Great Journey west.
The order of wizards sent to Middle-earth.
The priceless jewel at the heart of Thorin's claim to Erebor.
The Lonely Mountain, reclaimed from Smaug in The Hobbit.
The dragon who seized Erebor and its treasure.
The archer of Lake-town who kills Smaug.
Lake-town, the settlement built on the Long Lake.
The dark fortress in Mirkwood once occupied by Sauron.
The horse Aragorn rides in the films after falling over the cliff.
The men from Harad who fight for Sauron.
The northern realm once ruled by the Witch-king.
The volcano where the One Ring was forged and must be destroyed.
Bilbo's memoir title and a perfect summary of The Hobbit.
Tolkien's lore-heavy book of Middle-earth's ancient history.
The mountain pass that forces the Fellowship away from the Redhorn Gate.
The Elvish word for 'friend' that opens the Doors of Durin.
One of Thorin's dwarves in The Hobbit, known for his massive presence.
The famously heavy and sleepy dwarf from Thorin's company.
Tony Stark, the genius billionaire who built the first suit in a cave.
The web-slinging hero from Queens who was bitten by a radioactive spider.
Steve Rogers, the supersoldier with the vibranium shield.
Bruce Banner's giant green alter ego powered by rage.
The Asgardian god of thunder who wields Mjolnir.
Natasha Romanoff, the former spy and elite Avenger.
Clint Barton, the Avenger known for impossible shots with a bow.
The cosmic villain who wiped out half of all life with the Infinity Stones.
The sorcerer who protects Earth from magical and multiversal threats.
T'Challa, the king of Wakanda and its superhuman protector.
The synthetic Avenger created from JARVIS, Vibranium, and an Infinity Stone.
The foul-mouthed antihero who constantly breaks the fourth wall.
The mutant with claws, healing powers, and an adamantium skeleton.
Charles Xavier, the telepathic founder of the X-Men.
The mutant master of magnetism and frequent enemy of the X-Men.
Thor's trickster brother who keeps bouncing between villain and ally.
Peter Quill, the part-human leader of the Guardians of the Galaxy.
The tree-like Guardian whose most famous line is 'I am Groot.'
The weapons-loving Guardian who looks like a raccoon and hates being called one.
The hero who can shrink, grow, and fight using Pym Particles.
Carol Danvers, one of Marvel's most powerful cosmic heroes.
The one-eyed spy chief who assembled the Avengers.
The most famous Marvel creator and king of the cameo.
Marvel's flagship superhero team made up of Earth's mightiest heroes.
The mutant superhero team centered on Xavier's school.
Marvel's first family of superheroes: Reed, Sue, Johnny, and Ben.
The ragtag cosmic team led by Star-Lord.
The intelligence agency that deals with superhuman and alien threats.
The secret fascist organization with the motto 'Cut off one head, two more shall take its place.'
The six cosmic stones that control fundamental aspects of existence.
The glove built to hold all six Infinity Stones at once.
The moment Thanos erased half the universe with a finger snap.
Marvel's web of alternate realities and different versions of the same characters.
The rare Wakandan metal used in Captain America's shield and Black Panther's gear.
The nearly indestructible metal bonded to Wolverine's skeleton.
Thor's enchanted hammer, usable only by someone worthy.
Thor's axe-hammer weapon forged after Mjolnir was destroyed.
The glowing power source that kept Tony Stark alive and powered his suit.
The blue cube that housed the Space Stone.
Doctor Strange's relic, which in the MCU held the Time Stone.
The secretive African nation with advanced tech and vast Vibranium reserves.
Thor's home realm and the seat of Odin's rule.
Tony Stark's New York skyscraper, later turned into Avengers Tower.
The microscopic dimension tied to Ant-Man and time-travel tricks in Endgame.
Doctor Strange's magical headquarters in New York.
Captain America's best friend, later brainwashed into becoming the Winter Soldier.
Tony Stark's partner and longtime CEO of Stark Industries.
The fan-favorite S.H.I.E.L.D. agent whose death helped unite the Avengers.
The rogue AI who decided humanity itself was the problem.
Spider-Man villain Otto Octavius, known for his mechanical tentacles.
The alien symbiote antihero most famously linked to Eddie Brock.
The blue mutant shapeshifter associated with Magneto's side of the X-Men world.
The powerful telepath and telekinetic tied to the Phoenix Force.
The X-Man who can control the weather.
Galactus's cosmic herald who rides a silver board through space.
Frank Castle, the vigilante who wages a one-man war on crime.
Matt Murdock, the blind lawyer and street-level hero of Hell's Kitchen.
Wilson Fisk, the crime boss who often serves as Daredevil's main enemy.
The flaming-skulled spirit of vengeance on a motorcycle.
The half-vampire hunter who fights the undead with swords.
Kamala Khan, the New Jersey fangirl who became a hero herself.
The younger Spider-Man who became a star in the Spider-Verse era.
Thor's sister and the goddess of death in Thor: Ragnarok.
The living planet who turned out to be Star-Lord's father.
The blue Ravager who controls his arrow with a whistle.
Thanos's cybernetically enhanced daughter and Gamora's sister.
Thanos's deadliest daughter and a core member of the Guardians.
The literal-minded, heavily muscled Guardian seeking revenge on Thanos.
The empathic Guardian who can sense and affect emotions.
Thor's scientist love interest who later became Mighty Thor.
Doctor Strange's ally who eventually became Sorcerer Supreme.
The nearly immortal beings created by the Celestials.
The godlike cosmic beings behind much of Marvel's deep mythology.
The homeworld of the Nova Corps in the Guardians corner of Marvel.
The Greek king of the gods, known for thunderbolts and endless affairs.
The Roman counterpart to Zeus and ruler of the sky.
The Greek god of the sea, earthquakes, and the trident.
The Roman god of the sea.
The Greek god of the underworld and the dead.
The Roman ruler of the underworld and hidden wealth underground.
The Greek goddess of wisdom and strategic warfare.
The Roman goddess of wisdom, learning, and crafts.
The Greek god of violent, blood-soaked war.
The Roman god of war and one of Rome's most important deities.
The Greek goddess of love and beauty.
The Roman goddess of love, beauty, and desire.
The god of light, music, prophecy, and healing.
The Greek goddess of the hunt and the moon.
The Roman goddess of the hunt and wild places.
The Greek messenger god with winged sandals.
The Roman messenger god tied to speed, trade, and travel.
The Greek blacksmith god who forged divine weapons.
The Roman god of fire and metalworking.
The Greek god of wine, ecstasy, and wild celebration.
The Roman name associated with the god of wine.
The Roman name for Heracles, famed for his twelve labors.
The greatest Greek warrior in the Trojan War, with his famous vulnerable heel.
The king of Ithaca whose journey home took ten years after Troy fell.
The hero who beheaded Medusa and rescued Andromeda.
The Gorgon whose gaze turned people to stone.
The bull-headed monster trapped in the Labyrinth on Crete.
The three-headed dog that guards the underworld.
The container that unleashed the world's evils but left hope behind.
The Titan who stole fire from the gods for humanity.
The boy who flew too close to the sun on wax wings.
The Titan condemned to hold up the heavens.
The man forced to push a boulder uphill forever.
The ruler whose touch turned everything into gold.
The beautiful youth who fell in love with his own reflection.
The musician who journeyed into the underworld for Eurydice.
The daughter of Demeter whose yearly return explains the seasons.
The underworld river by which the gods swore binding oaths.
The ferryman who carries souls across the river of the dead.
The deepest, darkest prison of the underworld.
The paradise of the blessed dead and great heroes.
The mountain home of the major Greek gods.
The riddle-asking creature with a lion's body and human head.
The winged horse born from Medusa's blood.
The wise centaur who taught many Greek heroes.
The many-headed serpent that grew new heads when one was cut off.
The legendary prize sought by Jason and the Argonauts.
The band of heroes who sailed with Jason on the Argo.
The twin brothers raised by a she-wolf and linked to the founding of Rome.
The Greek goddess of victory.
The Titan father of Zeus who swallowed his own children.
The Roman counterpart to Kronos.
The primordial Greek goddess of the Earth.
The primordial Greek sky god and mate of Gaia.
The Greek goddess of agriculture and mother of Persephone.
The Roman goddess of grain and farming.
The Greek goddess of the hearth and home.
The Roman goddess of the hearth, served by the Vestal Virgins.
The Greek god of desire and attraction.
The Roman winged god of love with the bow and arrows.
The two-faced Roman god of doorways, transitions, and beginnings.
The wild Greek god of shepherds, flocks, and rustic music.
The goddess associated with witchcraft, crossroads, and the night.
The rainbow messenger goddess.
The personification of death.
The personification of sleep.
The dream figure associated with shaping dreams.
The goddess of retribution against pride and arrogance.
The sun god who drove the sun chariot across the sky.
The Greek goddess of the dawn.
The Titan associated with the vast world-encircling river.
The hero who led the quest for the Golden Fleece.
The sorceress who helped Jason and later took terrible revenge.
The Athenian hero who killed the Minotaur.
The hero who rode Pegasus and killed the Chimera.
The fire-breathing monster made of parts of multiple beasts.
The swift huntress who was nearly impossible to beat in a race.
The tragic king who fulfilled a prophecy by killing his father and marrying his mother.
The Greek king who led the Greek forces at Troy.
The husband of Helen whose stolen wife helped trigger the Trojan War.
The woman whose beauty launched a thousand ships.
Troy's greatest defender and prince.
The Trojan prince who carried off Helen and helped start the war.
The aged king of Troy during the Trojan War.
Achilles' closest companion, whose death pushed Achilles back into battle.
The Trojan hero who escaped the fall of Troy and became central to Roman myth.
The queen of Carthage linked to Aeneas in Roman legend.
Odysseus's faithful wife, who kept his suitors waiting for years.
The Cyclops whom Odysseus tricked by calling himself Nobody.
The deadly singers who lured sailors to their doom.
A wild woodland creature associated with revelry and lust.
A tree nymph tied to forests and woods.
A nymph associated with fresh water like springs and rivers.
The three-pronged spear of Poseidon or Neptune.
The famous sanctuary where Apollo's priestess delivered prophecies.
The personification of deep darkness.
The legendary nation of warrior women.
The Amazon queen whose belt became one of Heracles' labors.
The filthy stables Heracles had to clean in one of his labors.
The mighty Greek hero second only to Achilles in brute strength.
The arrogant pride that so often brings down heroes in Greek myth.
The National Basketball Association, the top league in men's pro basketball.
The championship series that decides the league title.
The trophy awarded to the NBA champion.
One half of the NBA playoff bracket, opposite the Western Conference.
The other NBA conference, traditionally stacked with many of the league's power teams.
The high-flying shot where a player throws the ball directly through the rim.
A made shot from beyond the three-point line.
A stat line with double digits in three major categories like points, rebounds, and assists.
A quick attack up the floor before the defense can get set.
The countdown that forces a team to shoot within a set number of seconds.
A shot that goes in just as the game or quarter-ending buzzer sounds.
The play where one player throws the ball near the rim and another catches and scores in midair.
The two-man action built around a screen and a drive or pass.
The dribble move used to fake one direction and break the defender the other way.
A key bench player who is often the first substitute and a major scorer.
The league's Most Valuable Player award.
The award for the best first-year player.
The midseason showcase featuring the league's top stars.
The system that determines the order of the top picks in the NBA Draft.
The event where teams select top incoming players, usually from college or overseas.
The spending system that limits how much teams can pay their rosters.
The largest contract a player can sign under NBA rules.
The penalty teams pay for spending too far above the salary cap.
The iconic New York arena often called the Mecca of basketball.
The Chicago arena forever linked to the Jordan-era Bulls.
The Los Angeles arena long associated with Lakers and Clippers basketball.
The glamour franchise of Los Angeles, tied to stars, titles, and celebrity culture.
Boston's historic franchise with one of the richest championship traditions in sports.
Chicago's franchise, defined forever by the Jordan dynasty.
San Antonio's disciplined dynasty built around Tim Duncan and Gregg Popovich.
The team that reshaped modern basketball around elite three-point shooting.
New York's franchise, famous for its market size and loyal fan base despite long frustration.
Miami's franchise associated with Pat Riley, Dwyane Wade, and the Big Three era.
Detroit's franchise, especially remembered for the Bad Boys era.
Philadelphia's franchise linked to names like Wilt, Dr. J, and Iverson.
Houston's franchise best known for Hakeem, titles, and star guards.
The six-time champion whose peak set the standard for basketball greatness.
The superstar forward whose longevity and all-around game fuel constant GOAT debate.
The Lakers legend known for five titles and the Mamba mentality.
The dominant big man who overpowered defenses in his prime.
The smiling Lakers point guard who led Showtime in the 1980s.
The trash-talking Celtics legend whose rivalry with Magic helped save the league.
The skyhook master and all-time great center.
The shooter who changed how modern basketball is played.
The quietly dominant Spurs big man known as the Big Fundamental.
The seven-foot scorer with guard skills and effortless offense.
The giant scorer famous for his 100-point game.
The Celtics legend whose championship total still towers over the sport.
The undersized superstar known for fearless scoring and crossover culture.
The elegant Rockets center famous for the Dream Shake.
The Mavericks legend who made the one-legged fadeaway iconic.
The intense two-way big man famous for energy, defense, and trash talk.
The Heat legend who starred before and during the Big Three era.
The all-around star whose triple-double seasons were once considered untouchable.
The Hall of Fame point guard who led the Bad Boy Pistons.
Dr. J, the stylish high-flying star who brought flair to pro basketball.
The coach who won titles with both the Bulls and Lakers.
The Spurs coach known for sustained excellence and blunt interviews.
The legendary Celtics coach and executive forever linked to cigar-smoking victory.
The slick coach-executive tied to Showtime and later the Miami Heat.
The 1992 U.S. Olympic team stacked with NBA legends.
The documentary series about Jordan's final Bulls season.
Jordan's legendary sick-game performance in the 1997 Finals.
The infamous Pacers-Pistons brawl involving players and fans.
Jeremy Lin's sudden scoring explosion with the Knicks.
The nickname for Steph Curry and Klay Thompson as a shooting duo.
The nickname for the Clippers era built around alley-oops and highlights.
The fast, flashy 1980s Lakers led by Magic Johnson.
The rough, physical Detroit teams that battled Jordan's Bulls.
The famous offensive system associated with Phil Jackson's title teams.
A defensive scheme where players guard areas instead of specific matchups.
A pressure defense applied over the entire length of the court.
The strategy of intentionally fouling a poor free-throw shooter.
The late part of a blowout when the result is basically decided.
Playing games on consecutive days.
The all-or-nothing final game of a seven-game playoff series.
The modern practice of resting stars to manage wear and tear.
A player who spends one college season before entering the NBA Draft.
The New York outdoor court famous for basketball legends and streetball culture.
The period when players can sign with new teams and reshape the league.
The championship game of the NFL and one of the biggest TV events in the United States.
The trophy awarded to the Super Bowl champion.
The National Football League, the top level of American football.
One of the NFL's two conferences, opposite the NFC.
The conference that sends its champion to face the AFC champion in the Super Bowl.
The main way to score in football, worth six points.
A kick through the uprights worth three points.
A desperate long pass thrown at the end of a game.
The area inside the opponent's 20-yard line where offenses are close to scoring.
The parking-lot pregame party culture around NFL games.
The position that leads the offense and throws most passes.
The ball carrier who runs between or outside the tackles and catches passes too.
The pass catcher lined up near the sidelines.
The hybrid position that blocks like a lineman and catches like a receiver.
A key defensive position that tackles runners, covers receivers, and blitzes quarterbacks.
When the quarterback is tackled behind the line of scrimmage.
When a defender catches a pass intended for the offense.
When a player loses control of the football while live.
The extra period played when a game is tied at the end of regulation.
The long-running prime-time NFL showcase on Monday nights.
The prime-time Sunday NFL game that usually gets one of the week's biggest matchups.
The tradition of NFL games played every Thanksgiving Day.
The game where fans draft real NFL players and compete based on their stats.
The annual event where teams select top college players.
The scouting event where prospects are measured, tested, and interviewed.
The iconic football video game series named after John Madden.
The historic home of the Green Bay Packers, known as the Frozen Tundra.
The famously loud home stadium of the Kansas City Chiefs.
The long-time home of the Chicago Bears.
The New Orleans dome stadium tied closely to Saints football.
The franchise that calls itself America's Team.
The small-market franchise with a massive legacy and fan ownership structure.
One of the league's most successful franchises, famous for black and gold and the Terrible Towel.
The dynasty franchise built around Tom Brady and Bill Belichick.
The franchise associated with Joe Montana, Jerry Rice, and multiple dynastic eras.
The modern contender powered by Patrick Mahomes and a huge fan culture.
One of the league's oldest franchises, tied forever to defense and tradition.
The silver-and-black franchise long associated with outlaw swagger.
The passionate Philly franchise that finally won its first Super Bowl in 2018.
The historic New York franchise that twice upset Brady's Patriots in the Super Bowl.
The franchise behind the NFL's only perfect season.
The franchise tied to John Elway, orange uniforms, and Mile High football.
The team that famously reached four straight Super Bowls.
The tough defensive franchise closely linked to Ray Lewis.
The purple franchise known for its long history and near-misses.
The 49ers legend whose cool under pressure made him one of the all-time greats.
The quarterback with seven Super Bowl titles and the strongest GOAT claim in football.
The cerebral star quarterback famous for audibles and pre-snap control.
The Chiefs superstar known for impossible throws and early-career dominance.
The wide receiver many still consider the greatest ever.
The deep-threat receiver who made 'You got Mossed' part of football language.
The NFL's all-time leader in rushing yards.
The electrifying Lions running back famous for impossible cuts.
Sweetness, the Bears legend known for power, grace, and versatility.
The dominant Browns running back often ranked among the greatest players ever.
The Giants pass rusher who changed what teams expected from linebackers.
The Hall of Fame defensive end nicknamed the Minister of Defense.
The fiery linebacker who became the face of Ravens defense.
Broadway Joe, famous for guaranteeing and winning Super Bowl III.
The Dolphins quarterback whose arm and stats made him a legend without a ring.
The gunslinging Packers quarterback known for toughness and improvisation.
The quarterback known for elite accuracy and low-interception seasons.
Prime Time, the flashy lockdown corner and return threat.
Gronk, the dominant tight end and Brady's favorite target for years.
The coach who built the Patriots dynasty with Brady.
The coaching icon whose name is now on the Super Bowl trophy.
The Super Bowl-winning coach who became football's most recognizable broadcaster.
The company that gave football its slow-motion mythmaking voice and style.
The famous Franco Harris playoff play that became part of Steelers legend.
Joe Montana to Dwight Clark, one of the defining moments in NFL history.
David Tyree's impossible catch that helped the Giants upset the Patriots.
The controversial ruling in the Brady-Raiders playoff game.
The nearly perfect New England team that lost the Super Bowl to the Giants.
The 1972 Dolphins' undefeated championship season, still unmatched.
The Patriots controversy over underinflated footballs.
Marshawn Lynch's tackle-breaking playoff touchdown run for Seattle.
The quick-passing offensive system made famous by Bill Walsh's 49ers.
A defensive scheme with two deep safeties splitting the field.
The hurry-up offense teams use late in a half when time is short.
A playoff spot awarded to a team that did not win its division.
The 8-bit Nintendo console that helped revive the video game industry in the 1980s.
Nintendo's classic 16-bit console, home to many of its most beloved games.
The cartridge-based console known for Mario 64, GoldenEye, and four-controller couch play.
Nintendo's purple cube-shaped console from the early 2000s.
The motion-control console that became a mainstream family phenomenon.
Nintendo's hybrid system that works both as a handheld and a home console.
The handheld that made portable gaming huge with games like Tetris and Pokemon.
Nintendo's infamous red-and-black 3D headset system that flopped fast.
Nintendo's mascot plumber in red who stars in the company's biggest series.
Mario's taller, greener, and more nervous brother.
The spiky-shelled King of the Koopas and Mario's main villain.
The ruler of the Mushroom Kingdom who is often kidnapped by Bowser.
Mario's dinosaur sidekick who can eat enemies with his tongue.
Mario's greedy rival with a yellow cap and his own chaotic games.
Luigi's lanky purple rival, mostly known from spinoff games.
The cosmic princess introduced in the Super Mario Galaxy games.
The mushroom-headed helper seen all across the Mario series.
The small brown mushroom-like enemy that Mario stomps constantly.
One of Bowser's turtle-like foot soldiers.
The snapping plant enemy that pops out of pipes.
The hero of The Legend of Zelda, known for his sword, shield, and green roots.
The princess of Hyrule whose name titles the series, not Link's.
The armored bounty hunter from Metroid, revealed to be a woman in the first game.
The dragon-like space pirate and recurring nemesis of Samus.
Nintendo's sci-fi action series starring Samus Aran.
Nintendo's famous ape who started as Mario's original arcade rival.
Donkey Kong's smaller, cap-wearing sidekick.
The pink puffball who inhales enemies and copies their powers.
Fox McCloud, the leader of Star Fox.
Falco Lombardi, Fox's cocky bird teammate.
The signature fighter ship piloted by the Star Fox team.
The F-Zero racer known as much for 'Falcon Punch' as for driving.
The baseball-bat-wielding kid protagonist of EarthBound.
The blue-haired Fire Emblem swordsman many players first met in Smash Bros.
Nintendo's crossover fighting series where its biggest characters battle each other.
Nintendo's racing series known for chaos, shortcuts, and ruined friendships.
The Mario Kart item that punishes whoever is in first place.
The colorful, infamous Mario Kart track with no forgiveness.
The cloud-riding turtle who waves the start lights and fishes racers out of pits.
Mario's puzzle-game alter ego in a white lab coat.
The RPG spinoff series where Mario looks like a flat paper cutout.
The landmark 3D platformer that set the standard for the genre.
The Zelda game often cited as one of the greatest video games ever made.
The darker Zelda game built around a repeating three-day countdown.
The open-world Zelda game that reinvented the series on Switch.
The falling-block puzzle classic that became inseparable from the Game Boy.
Nintendo's fantasy adventure series set in Hyrule.
The handheld successor to the Game Boy Color with a wide landscape design.
The dual-screen handheld with a touch screen and one of the best-selling systems ever.
Nintendo's board-game party series built around mini-games and betrayal.
Mario's most famous catchphrase.
The physicist behind relativity and the equation E = mc^2.
The scientist famous for the laws of motion and universal gravitation.
The theoretical physicist known for black hole work and A Brief History of Time.
The pioneering physicist and chemist who helped uncover radioactivity.
The physicist whose atomic model became a standard stepping stone in quantum theory.
Werner Heisenberg, best known for the uncertainty principle.
Erwin Schrodinger, the physicist behind wave mechanics and the famous cat thought experiment.
Richard Feynman, the brilliant physicist known for QED and his unforgettable teaching style.
James Clerk Maxwell, whose equations unified electricity and magnetism.
Nikola Tesla, the inventor and engineer associated with alternating current.
Michael Faraday, the experimental genius behind electromagnetic induction.
Max Planck, the founder of quantum theory.
The early scientist who transformed astronomy and the study of motion.
J. Robert Oppenheimer, the scientific leader of the Manhattan Project.
Hendrik Lorentz, whose transformations became crucial to relativity.
Christiaan Huygens, the Dutch physicist tied to wave theory and Saturn's rings.
Ludwig Boltzmann, a key figure in statistical mechanics.
Enrico Fermi, the physicist behind major advances in nuclear and particle physics.
Paul Dirac, the theorist who predicted antimatter.
The ancient Greek thinker famous for buoyancy and mechanical insight.
Johannes Kepler, known for the laws describing planetary motion.
Blaise Pascal, whose name lives on in pressure and probability.
Lord Kelvin, whose name is attached to the absolute temperature scale.
Andre-Marie Ampere, whose name is used for electric current.
Georg Ohm, known for the law relating voltage, current, and resistance.
The unit of power, named after James Watt.
The unit of energy, named after James Prescott Joule.
The unit of frequency, named after Heinrich Hertz.
Christian Doppler, whose name is attached to the shift caused by relative motion.
The force that pulls masses toward each other.
Einstein's framework for space, time, gravity, and motion at high speeds.
The branch of physics that describes matter and energy at tiny scales.
The study of heat, energy, work, and how they relate.
A measure of disorder or of how spread out energy is in a system.
The cosmic speed limit at which light travels in a vacuum.
A region of space where gravity is so strong that not even light escapes.
The leading theory for how the universe began in a hot, dense state.
The quantum idea that a system can exist in multiple states at once until measured.
The quantum link that makes separated particles behave in connected ways.
An object's tendency to resist changes in its motion.
The force that resists motion between surfaces in contact.
The splitting of a heavy atomic nucleus into smaller parts.
The joining of light nuclei, the process that powers stars like the Sun.
The lowest possible temperature, where thermal motion is minimized.
Matter made of particles with opposite charge to ordinary matter.
The invisible stuff thought to make up much of the universe's mass.
The particle tied to the Higgs field, which gives mass to other particles.
The boundary around a black hole beyond which nothing can return.
The spontaneous decay of unstable atomic nuclei.
The particle of light.
The negatively charged particle found around an atom's nucleus.
The positively charged particle inside an atomic nucleus.
The neutral particle found alongside protons in the nucleus.
A fundamental particle that combines to form protons and neutrons.
Einstein's equation linking mass and energy.
Newton's law stating that force equals mass times acceleration.
The formula for weight near Earth's surface.
The formula for linear momentum.
Ohm's law relating voltage, current, and resistance.
The electrical formula for power.
The formula for work as force applied through a distance.
The basic formula for speed or velocity.
The relation between a photon's energy and its frequency.
The ideal gas law linking pressure, volume, temperature, and amount of gas.
The constant that appears in Newton's law of gravitation.
The fundamental constant that sets the scale of quantum effects.
A version of an element with the same number of protons but a different number of neutrons.
The time it takes for half of a radioactive sample to decay.
The shift in observed frequency caused by motion between source and observer.
The state where a material carries electricity with zero resistance.
A measure of how much electric charge a system can store.
The field around a magnet or moving charge that affects other charges.
An ionized state of matter made of charged particles.
Mass per unit volume.
A material whose electrical behavior makes modern electronics possible.
The pattern produced when waves combine and reinforce or cancel each other.
The energy an object has because it is moving.
Stored energy due to position, arrangement, or state.
The class of particles that makes up matter, such as electrons and quarks.
The class of particles that carries forces, such as photons and gluons.
The speculative idea that fundamental particles are tiny vibrating strings.
A measurable property such as mass, time, temperature, or speed.
The agreed standard used to measure a physical quantity.
The electric mouse and global mascot of the franchise.
The dragon-like final evolution of Charmander and one of Pokemon's biggest fan favorites.
The genetically engineered psychic Pokemon created from Mew.
The Grass-type starter with the plant bulb on its back.
The turtle Water-type starter that eventually becomes Blastoise.
The anime's forever-young trainer from Pallet Town.
The professor who gives new trainers their first Pokemon and a Pokedex.
The criminal organization always trying to steal rare Pokemon.
The terrifying sea serpent evolved from the useless-looking Magikarp.
The giant sleepy Pokemon that often blocks the road.
The Pokemon famous for its many different evolutions.
The grinning Ghost-type with a shadowy vibe.
The Cerulean City Gym Leader and one of Ash's original anime companions.
The Pewter City Gym Leader who traveled with Ash for years.
The pink Pokemon that sings people to sleep and then draws on their faces.
The confused duck Pokemon with constant headaches and hidden psychic power.
The friendly but powerful dragon that evolves from Dragonair.
The Electric-type Legendary Bird.
The Ice-type Legendary Bird.
The Fire-type Legendary Bird.
The mythical pink Pokemon believed to contain the DNA of all Pokemon.
The standard device used to catch and store Pokemon.
The electronic encyclopedia that records information on Pokemon.
The rare ball with a guaranteed catch rate.
The hometown of Ash Ketchum and Gary Oak.
Ash's original rival and Professor Oak's grandson.
The boss of Team Rocket and Viridian City's Gym Leader.
The giant rock snake most associated with Brock.
Misty's iconic Water- and Psychic-type starfish Pokemon.
The large fire dog often described as a legendary-looking Pokemon.
The spoon-bending psychic evolved from Abra.
The four-armed Fighting-type powerhouse.
The gentle sea Pokemon used for transport across water.
The giant Psychic- and Flying-type legendary tied to the sea.
The rainbow-colored legendary bird that appears in the very first anime episode.
The purple transformation Pokemon that can copy other Pokemon.
The famously dim, slow-moving pink Pokemon.
The bladed mantis Pokemon known for speed and slicing attacks.
The ultra-rare promo card often called the holy grail of Pokemon cards.
The item that instantly raises a Pokemon's level by one.
The evolution stone used on Pokemon like Clefairy and Nidorina.
The spooky Kanto town best known for Pokemon Tower and its ghost stories.
The volcanic island tied to the creation of Mewtwo.
Squirtle's final evolution with giant water cannons.
Bulbasaur's final evolution, with a huge flower on its back.
Pikachu's stronger evolved form.
Team Rocket's talking cat Pokemon in the anime.
The psychic Pokemon that loves escaping with Teleport.
Abra's evolved form, still clutching a spoon.
The floating Ghost-type between Gastly and Gengar.
The lonely Pokemon that wears its dead mother's skull.
The kicking specialist named after Bruce Lee.
The punching specialist named after Jackie Chan.
The floating poison-ball Pokemon often used by Team Rocket.
Koffing's evolved form, made of linked poisonous gas sacs.
The snake Pokemon whose name is 'snake' backwards.
The cobra Pokemon whose name is 'kobra' backwards.
The Normal-type Pokemon with the absurdly long tongue.
The pink nurse-like Pokemon often seen helping in Pokemon Centers.
The anime character who runs Pokemon Centers.
The anime police officer who appears in town after town.
The famously weak fish Pokemon that evolves into Gyarados.
The baby pre-evolution of Pikachu.
The very common purple rodent from early-game Kanto.
The bird Pokemon almost everyone catches early in Red and Blue.
The horned powerhouse evolved from Nidorino.
The fairy-like Pokemon tied to the Moon Stone and Mt. Moon.
The small fox Pokemon with multiple tails.
Vulpix's elegant nine-tailed evolution.
The cave bat every player runs into far too often.
The little blue plant Pokemon that looks like a weed with feet.
The bug Pokemon controlled by the giant mushroom on its back.
The large moth Pokemon evolved from Venonat.
The tiny ground Pokemon that pops out of the earth.
Three Diglett stuck together as one faster, stronger evolution.
Meowth's sleek evolved form and Giovanni's signature Pokemon.
The furious evolved form of Mankey.
The tadpole Pokemon with the spiral belly.
The fighting-capable final evolution of Poliwag.
The rock Pokemon that is basically a boulder with fists.
The huge rocky final evolution of Geodude.
The horse Pokemon with a mane of fire.
The blazing, faster evolved form of Ponyta.
The floating magnet Pokemon.
Three Magnemite fused into one Electric-type cluster.
The duck Pokemon that carries a leek.
The three-headed evolved form of Doduo.
The icy sea lion Pokemon evolved from Seel.
The toxic sludge Pokemon.
The spiky shell evolution of Shellder.
The ghostly gas-ball that starts the Gastly line.
The psychic Pokemon with the pendulum and creepy reputation.
The crab Pokemon with one oversized pincer.
The Pokemon that looks like a Poké Ball and loves to self-destruct.
The faster, angrier evolution of Voltorb.
The cluster of psychic eggs that later becomes Exeggutor.
Cubone's bone-wielding evolved form.
The blue vine-covered Pokemon with hidden eyes.
The kangaroo-like Pokemon with a baby in its pouch.
Horsea's spikier evolved form.
The spinning starfish Pokemon tied to Misty.
The barrier-making mime Pokemon.
The Ice- and Psychic-type with the singing, dancing style.
The striped Electric-type that looks like a walking power plant.
The fire duck-like Pokemon with a lava-colored body.
The beetle Pokemon with huge pincer horns.
The polygonal digital Pokemon created by humans.
The scythe-armed fossil Pokemon evolved from Kabuto.
The fierce prehistoric flying fossil Pokemon.
The rare dragon Pokemon that starts the Dragonite line.
Dratini's elegant serpentine evolution.
The Pokemon researcher behind the PC storage system.
The Kanto cave known for Clefairy, Zubat, and Moon Stones.
The big Kanto city with the department store, casino, and Erika's Gym.
The site of the Pokemon League and Elite Four in Kanto.
A fan term for one of Eevee's many evolved forms.
A stronger Poké Ball that sits below the Ultra Ball.
A high-performance Poké Ball with a better catch rate than a Great Ball.
The basic healing item almost every player uses early on.
The item that brings a fainted Pokemon back into battle.
Han Solo's beat-up but legendary ship that supposedly made the Kessel Run in less than 12 parsecs.
The command that turned the clone troopers against the Jedi.
The powerful Jedi Master with the purple lightsaber.
The microscopic life-forms used to explain a person's connection to the Force.
Obi-Wan's master who discovered Anakin Skywalker on Tatooine.
The city-covered capital planet of the Republic and later the Empire.
The Sith identity of Emperor Palpatine.
The red-and-black Sith with the double-bladed lightsaber.
The former Jedi who became Darth Tyranus.
The cyborg general who collected Jedi lightsabers.
Lando Calrissian, the smooth-talking gambler and later Rebel leader.
The desert planet where Luke and Anakin both grew up.
The giant crime boss slug from Tatooine.
The forest moon where the Rebels fought the Empire with help from the Ewoks.
The small furry forest creatures from Return of the Jedi.
The Empire's moon-sized battle station with a planet-destroying superlaser.
The giant wedge-shaped capital ship of the Imperial fleet.
The Rebel Alliance's most famous starfighter.
Ben Solo, the conflicted dark-side warrior of the sequel trilogy.
The scavenger from Jakku who became the new Jedi lead of the sequel trilogy.
The stormtrooper who broke ranks and joined the Resistance.
The brave astromech droid who keeps saving everyone.
The anxious protocol droid fluent in millions of forms of communication.
Han Solo's towering Wookiee co-pilot and best friend.
The elegant home planet of Padme Amidala and Emperor Palpatine.
The clumsy Gungan from The Phantom Menace.
The Sith Lord from Palpatine's famous story about cheating death.
The masked desert nomads who roam Tatooine.
The hooded scavengers who travel Tatooine in sandcrawlers.
The huge moving junk vehicle used by the Jawas.
The material Han Solo was frozen in by the Empire.
The armored bounty hunter who became one of the franchise's coolest icons.
The bounty hunter whose DNA was used to create the clone army.
The era of the Jedi at their height, set long before the Skywalker saga.
The fear, anger, and hatred-fueled side of the Force.
A Jedi apprentice still training under a master.
A fully trained Jedi who has moved beyond apprenticeship.
A high-ranking Jedi recognized for great wisdom and skill.
The standard energy gun used across the galaxy.
The rough, far-flung edge of the galaxy where law and order are thin.
The Force technique used to plant a suggestion in someone's mind.
Episode VII, the film that launched the sequel trilogy.
Episode VIII, the sequel that split the fanbase the hardest.
Episode IX, the finale of the Skywalker saga.
The original 1977 film that started Star Wars.
The darker sequel where Darth Vader reveals his true relationship to Luke.
The movie where Luke faces Vader and the second Death Star falls.
Episode I, the prequel that introduced young Anakin and Darth Maul.
Episode II, where the Clone Wars begin and Anakin's fall deepens.
Episode III, where Anakin becomes Darth Vader.
The spinoff about the mission to steal the Death Star plans.
Princess Leia's home planet, destroyed by the Death Star.

word: May the Force be with you
explanation: The franchise's signature blessing and catchphrase.
The most famous reveal in the entire saga.
